text: It is generally thought that St Mark is here treating the space between nine o’clock and noon as a single stretch of time, which he calls ‘the third hour’; we are not, then, to suppose that our Lord was nailed to his cross at nine o’clock, and hung six hours upon it. If he was crucified at eleven, or even half-past eleven, it would still be during ‘the third hour’, in the sense that ‘the sixth hour’ had not yet begun (cf.v. 33 below). A further difficulty is raised upon the question of time by Jn. 19.14; see p. 138, note 4.

The body of a man who had been crucified must be taken down and buried before night-fall (Deut. 21.23). But here early action was necessary, because it was a Friday, and after six o’clock in the evening the sabbath would have begun, so that the work of burial would have become unlawful.
